results.possibleIdentity: George E. Adamy (born 1932) is a technical expert, artist, and educator who specialized in the use of plastics and acrylic resins in art and construction. He is the developer of the "Polyadam" system (also known as PII), a high-quality acrylic polymer additive designed to be combined with portland cement and glass scrim to create strong, lightweight, and weather-resistant structures. Adamy operated through the GEA Company, which was based in Larchmont, New York. During the late 1960s and 1970s, he was an active member of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) community, providing technical expertise to artists in fields such as acrylics, sculpture, and the creation of "environments." His own creative work includes cast acrylic resin sculptures, such as his 1969 piece "Who's Afraid of Rose Mr. Newman?" He was recognized as an educator in his field, teaching classes in plastics and jewelry making to other artists in the New York area.
